title: Java Pattern Switch
description: Match types and guards directly in a switch expression.
learningGoals: Select a branch by type using pattern labels in a switch expression.
Recall that guarded patterns, null handling, and dominant cases affect which labels are legal.

completionChecks.prompt: What does the when clause do in case Integer i when i < 0?
choices: Adds a guard that must also be true
Makes the case run after default
completionChecks.explanation: The when clause is a guard; the pattern must match and the guard must evaluate true for that case to apply.

completionChecks.prompt: Why is case null written explicitly here?
choices: default must always appear before null
A null selector is not handled by the other type patterns
completionChecks.explanation: Type patterns do not match null, so an explicit case null handles that selector value before default.
The Integer pattern matches first and its when guard is true, so the unguarded Integer case is skipped.
